54. Sakalas. 6. 63. Sphotayana. P.
55. Sakalya. &. K. P. 64. Harita. T.

For the Saraa-veda no Pratisakhya has as yet been discovered. There is a small treatise which I found in the same manuscript of the Bodleian Library which contains the Taittiriy a- pratisakhya, and which might be called a Pratisakhya of the Sama-veda. But it is so badly written, and so unintelligible without a commentary, that little use can be made of it at present. It is called Sama-tantra \ and evidently treats of the same subjects which usually occur in the Prati- 1 It begins (MS. Bodl. W. 505.) ^TW^TT^ *\WA\ W^tf- f^t^i w^i wethii ^Tin wi *rai f^ffaf%i fwi Xf^fi<y4u ^ttt w ^i ^m i^rrei ^<2J| 7[fTT I f^JTT II &c. From my notes taken in the Royal Library of Berlin, I see that the same work exists there with a commentary (?) in 13 Prapathakas. ^^rUFf^F^ st^fYT^Nf^T ^TTft^Tf5T^T*f ^TT3fTW I- Tlie same work I find mentioned H4 PRATISAKHYAS.
sakhyas. Its authenticity is supported by the Charanavyiiha, where a Sama-tantra is mentioned, but without any further particulars.

If it be asked now why all these works, so different in appearance, are to be ascribed to one period of literature, the Sutra-period, the reasons for it are as follows : first, that the style of the majority of these works is the old Sutra style, for instance, in the Taittiriya-pratisakhya, the Katyayaniya-pratisakhya, and the Chaturadhyayika1 ; secondly, that the manuscripts call these works Sutras; thirdly, that even works, written in mixed Slokas, like those of Saunaka, are quoted as Sutras2, a title which would never be given to works like the Manava-dharmasastra, &c. ; and fourthly, that the same men to whom these works are ascribed are known to have composed other works, generally written in the style of in Dr. Weber's interesting article on the Sama-veda. (Indische Studien, i. 48.) It is curious that this Samatantra is called Vyakarana, grammar. The same name is also given to the Riktantra, a imall Siksha treatise, MS. Bodl. W. 375. This MS. contains several small treatises on 6iksha matters connected with the Sama-veda, but more in the form of Parisishtas : one on Avagraha, or division of words; another called Samasankhya; and a third called Stobhanusanhara, beginning with the words 1 The title put at the end of the chapters of the Taittiriyapratisakhya is u iti pratisakhya-sutre prathamah prasnah samaptah, &c."

2 Shadgurusishya, in his Commentary on the Anukramani, says that 6aunaka first composed a Kalpa-sutra, consisting of 1000 parts and resembling a Brahmana. ^^ftcf^ ^^rf ~^k ^V^W^" f%*f. This was afterwards destroyed by himself; but his few remaining works, which are written in verse, are equally called Sutras, ^pl^3F*l PRATISAKHYAS. 145 Sutras. That the Pratisakhya of the Sakalas should be written in Slokas and yet be ascribed to Saunaka, the teacher of Katyayana, is no objection. It would have to be excluded from the Sutra period, if written in regular Anushtubh-slokas like those of Manu. But the mixture of the Sloka with other ancient metres indicates better than anything else the transition from one period to another, and is quite in accordance with that position which, as will be seen, Saunaka occupies in the literary history of India.

By comparing Saunaka's chapters on Siksha in his first Pratisakhya with the small Sloka compilation which is generally quoted as the Yedanga, the difference of old and modern Slokas will at once be perceived. This modern tract which has been printed in India, contains scarcely more than the matter of the Siva or Samkara-sutras brought into Slokas. It mentions the Prakrit dialects, and represents itself as written after Panini, but not, as Madhusudana Sarasvati pretends, by Panini.1 Yet it is curious to see how great a reputation this small work must have gained, because Sayana, who knows the Pratisakhyas and quotes both from the Sakala and Taittiriya-pratisakhya, regards this small tract as the real Vedanga. In a Mimansa work, which has been mentioned before, Somesvara's Tantra- and again :
varttika-tika, it seems even as if greater authority had been attributed to this short Siksha tract than to the more developed and evidently older works of Saunaka, Katyayana, and others.

Besides these works on Siksha which have been enumerated, from the Taittiriyaranyaka down to the so-called Vedanga, we possess another tract on Siksha, called the Manduki-siksha.1 But this also is probably a production later than the Sutra period, and it is important only in so far as it bears the name of another Charana of the Rig-veda, theMandukayanas2, and thus confirms what was pointed out before, that each of the old Sakhas had originally its own Pratisakhya, although the greater number of them, as well as 1 Another work on 6iksha is mentioned by Raja Radhakanta in the article which he has dedicated to the Vedangas in his 6abda-ka1pa-drumn, and for which Amara and Bharata are quoted as authorities, rp^ ^^CTf^WT«Tf W«HTTWT?^'€Y- f^TT ^ of TT ^ fwsHfaT: 3fhlT T<4|lR*T far^T The Commentary on the &akala-pratisakhya also seems to speak of two Sikshas. ^JT fTT^f^^T^lf ^"*T^?T ^?^TT* 2 Mandukeya is quoted in the &akala-pratisakhya, I. 200.
CHHANDAS. 147 their Mantra texts, are now lost or preserved only under a more modern form, as may be seen in the case of this Manduki-siksha.
Chhandas, or Metre.

The second Yedanga doctrine, Chhandas or metre, stands very much in the same position as the Siksha. Some names which have been afterwards adopted as the technical designations of metres, occur in some of the Mantras of the Rig-veda, and there are frequent allusions to metres in the Brahmanas. What is said, however, in the Brahmanas with reference to metres, is generally so full of dogmatic and mystical ingredients as to be of scarcely any practical use. In the Aranyakas and Upanishads whole chapters are devoted to this subject. Yet it is again in the Sutras only that a real attempt has been made to arrange these archaic metres systematically. We have some chapters on metres at the end of the Sakala-pratisakhya, written in Saunaka's usual style of mixed Slokas. This treatise is anterior to that of Katyayana which we find in the introduction to his Sarvanukrama, because Katyayana is the pupil of Saunaka, as we shall see hereafter. For the metres of the Sama-veda we have the Nidana-sutra in ten prapathakas, which, after explaining the nature and different names of all the Vedic metres, gives a kind of index (anukramani) to the metres as they occur in the hymns employed at the Ekaha, Ahina, and Sattra sacrifices. As to Pingalanaga's work on Chhandas, which is most frequently quoted under the title of Yedanga, it does not pretend to be of greater antiquity than L 2 148 CHHANDAS.

the Mahabhashya, supposing it were admitted that Patanjali, the author of this famous commentary on Panini, was the same as Pingala.1 There would be nothing extraordinary in the fact that Pingala treats of Prakrit as well as Sanskrit metres. For we have the instance of Katyayana-Yararuchi, who wrote the Yarttikas on Panini and lived before Patanjali, and is said to be the same who wrote a grammar of the Prakrit dialects. It must be admitted, however, that Pingalanaga's Metric is one of the last works that could possibly be included in the Sutra period ; though there is no sufficient ground for excluding it from this period altogether, merely because those rules which refer to metres not yet employed in the Veda are ascribed to the same Pingala. Besides, Pingala is quoted as an authority on metres in the Parisishtas2, a class of literature which does not seem to be separated from the Sutra period by a long interval. To the same class of Chhandas works to which Pingala's treatise belongs, and which are not restricted to certain Sakhas, but are intended for the Veda in general, two other works are added by the commentator on the Sakala-pratisakhya, the one ascribed to Yaska, the other to Saitava.3 Both these works, however, seem to be lost at present.
1 Colebrooke, Miscellaneous Essays, ii. 63.

2 MS. Bodl. W. 466. *T*TTTTT W^l I sn^WTTff^lp* ^NjTTrT^HYw ^T^W I- See Dr. Roth's preface to the Nirukta, CHEIANDAS. 149 The difference between aChhandas work belonging to one of the Sakhas, and those treatises which are occupied with metre in general, may be seen from the following instance.

According to Pingala's Sutras, a metre of seventysix syllables is called Atidhriti, a metre of sixtyeight syllables Atyashti. Now Ev. i. 127, 6. a verse occurs of sixty-eight syllables which ought therefore to be called an Atyashti. According to Pingala himself, however, some syllables may be pronounced as two *, and if we follow his rules on this point, the same verse consists of seventy-six instead of sixty-eight syllables. In order, therefore, to remove the uncertainty attached to the metre of this verse, the Chhandas chapter in the Sakala-pratisakhya (towards the end of the 16th Patala) declares that according to the tradition of the Sakala or Saisira-sakha, this verse is to be pronounced as an Atidhriti, i. e. with seventy-six syllables. The same direction is given in Katyayana's index to the Sakala-sanhita.

Yyakarana, or Grammar.

The third Vedanga is Vyakarana or Grammar. According to the account which Indian authors give of their literature, this branch of Yedic learning would be represented by the Grammar of Panini. Here the contradiction becomes even more glaring. In Pingala's Sutras the Yedic metres were at least treated in the same way as the non-Vedic. But in Panini, the rules which refer to Yedic grammar in particular, form only the exceptions to all the other rules which treat of the regular or classical language. Instead, therefore, of considering the third Yedanga doctrine as represented by the grammarians beginning with Panini (Paninyadayah), as Indian authors do, it would be more correct to say that it is represented by the grammarians ending with Panini (Paninyantah). It unfortunately happened that Panini's work acquired by its great merits such a celebrity as to supersede almost all that had been written on grammar before him, so that, except the names and some particular rules of former grammarians, we have little left of this branch of literature, except what occurs occasionally in the Pratisakhyas, That Panini knew the Pratisakhyas had been indicated long ago by Professor Bohtlingk ; and it can be proved now by a comparison of Panini's Sutras with those of the Pratisakhyas, that Panini largely availed himself of the works of his predecessors, frequently adopting their very expressions, though he quotes their names only in cases where they have to serve as authorities for certain rules.

There are two separate treatises on grammatical vyAkahana. 151 subjects, which belong to a period anterior to Panini : the Sutras on the Unadi affixes, and the Sutras of Santanacharya on accents. The Unadi affixes are those by which nouns are formed from roots, the nouns being used in a conventional sense, and not in strict accordance with their radical meaning. They are called Unadi, because, in the Sutras as we now possess them, uji is the first-mentioned affix. That Panini was acquainted with the same arrangement of these formative affixes cannot be doubted, because he uses the same technical name (unadi) for them. We do not know by whom these Unadi affixes were first collected, nor by whom the Unadi-sutras, as we now possess them, were first composed. All we can say is, that, as Panini mentions them, and gives several general rules with regard to them, they must have existed before his time. But how many of the Sutras existed before the time of Panini, and how many were added afterwards, is a question that can hardly be solved. In their present form the Sutras seem to treat the Vedic words as exceptions, at least they give now and then a hint that a certain derivation applies to the Chhandas only. Nevertheless it is curious to observe that the greater number of words, explained by the genuine Unadi-sutras, are Vedic, some of them exclusively so. If the author of the Sutras had intended his rules for the Bhasha, there would have been no reason why he should have paid such prominent regard to words of a purely Vedic character. In fact, I believe, that originally the Unadi-sutras were intended for the Veda only, and that they were afterwards enlarged by adding rules on the formation of non- Vedic words. At last the non- Vedic or laukika words assumed such a prepon- i. 4 152 NIRUKTA.

derance that some rules, affecting Vedic words only, had actually to be inserted as exceptions. If a clear line could be drawn between words purely Vedic, and words never used in the Yeda, and if the Sutras referring to the former were separated from those of the latter class, we might perhaps arrive at the original text of this interesting work. This, however, is an undertaking which would require a more comprehensive and more critical knowledge of the history of the languages of India, than any scholar at present is likely to command.

As to Santana's Phitsutras, we know with less certainty to what period they belong. A knowledge of them is not presupposed by Panini, and the grammatical terms "used by Santana are different from those employed by Panini, — a fact from which Professor Bbhtlingk has ingeniously concluded, that Santana must have belonged to the eastern school of grammarians.1 As, however, these Sutras treat only of the accent, and the accent is used in the Vedic language only, the subject of Santana's work would lead us to suppose that he was anterior to Panini, though it would be unsafe to draw any further conclusions from this.
Nirukta, or Etymology.

The fourth Vedanga is Nirukta or Etymology. In the same way as, according to Indian authors, Grammar, as a Vedanga, was represented by Panini's Grammar, we find Nirukta also represented by but one work, generally known by the name of Yaska's 1 Cf. Bohtlingk, Ein erster Versuch iiber den Accent im Sanskrit, p. 64. ; and Panini, page xii.

NIRUKTA. 153 Nirukta. Nirukta, however, has had this advantage over Vyakarana, that Yaska's work applies itself exclusively to Vedic etymologies. In the same way as we considered Panini's Grammar as the work where Vyakarana, as a Vedanga, took its final shape, so Yaska also would seem to be one of the last authors who embodied the etymological lexicography of Vedic terms in one separate work. Niruktakaras, or authors of Niruktas, are mentioned by Yaska ; and some of them must have been as famous as Yaska himself, because we find that their merits in this respect were not forgotten even at the time of the compilation of the Puranas.1 For explanations of old Vedic words, for etymologies and synonymous expressions, the Brahmanas contain very rich materials, and, with the exception of the Kalpa, no other Vedanga has a better claim than the Nirukta to be considered as founded upon the Brahmanas. Whole verses and hymns are shortly explained there ; and the Aranyakas and Upanishads, if included, would furnish richer sources for Vedic etymologies than even the Nirukta itself. The beginning of the Aitareya-aranyaka is in fact a commentary on the 1 Thus 6akapurni is mentioned as a Niruktakrit in the Vishnupurana (p. 277. n. 9.) ; but this is no reason why 6akapurni should be the same as Yaska, as Colebrooke supposed. (Miscell. Essays, i. 15.) In fact 6akapuni is quoted by Yaska himself, for there can be no doubt that Sakapurni is the same name as Sakapuni. In later times, also, Yaska and 6akapuni are regarded as distinct persons ; for instance, in a verse ascribed to Parasara (Anukr. Bh.
iv. 5. 7.), which occurs in the Brihaddevata, JJA*H f%^f^?F Another Niruktakrit mentioned by Sayana is the son of Sthulashthivi, or, as Yaska calls him, Sthaulashthivi.

beginning of the Kig-veda ; and if all the passages of the Brahmanas were collected where one word is explained by another with which it is joined merely bv the particle vai, they would even now give a rich harvest for a new Nirukta. It is important, however, not to confound Yaska' s Nirukta with Yaska' s Commentary on the Nirukta, although it has become usual, after the fashion of modern manuscripts, to call that commentary Nirukta, and to distinguish the text of the Nirukta by the name of Nighantu. The* original Niruktas that formed an integral part of the Vedanga literature, known to Yaska himself, can have consisted only of lists of words arranged according to their meaning, like that upon which Yaska's Commentary is based. Whether the same Yaska who wrote the Commentary had some part in the arrangement of the lists of words, is unknown ; probably these lists existed in his family long before his time, as Yaska implies himself (Nir. i. 20.). But, as he preserved them by his Commentary, it was natural that their authorship, too, should have been ascribed to him. Sayana gives the following account of this matter: — " Nirukta is a work where a number of words is given, without any intention to connect them in a sentence. In that book, where a traditional number of words is taught, which begin with Gauh, gma, and end with Vasavah, Vajinah, Devapatnyah, there is no intention to state things which are to be understood *, because 1 If Sayana means to give in these lines an etymology instead of a simple definition of Nirukta, the attempt would be very unsuccessful. Nirukta comes from nirvach, to explain. His definition, however, is right, in so far as the Nirukta does not NIRUKTA. 155 it is only said there that l so many are the names of earth/ ' so many the names of gold,' " &c.
This Nirukta consists of three parts, as may be seen from the Commentary on the Anukramanika. Here we read :

55. Śākalya。&. K. P. 64. Hārīta。T.

關於《娑摩吠陀》（Sāma-veda），至今尚未發現任何闡釋書（Prātiśākhya）。我在博德利圖書館（Bodleian Library）同一份收錄《泰帝利耶闡釋書》（Taittirīya-prātiśākhya）的手稿中，找到一篇短論，或許可稱為《娑摩吠陀闡釋書》。然而，該文書寫極為拙劣，若無註解則難以理解，故目前幾乎無法利用。此篇名為《娑摩總論》（Sāma-tantra），顯然處理的是闡釋書（Prātiśākhya）中常見的主題。¹ 該文開頭（博德利手稿 W. 505）為：……（此處保留梵文原文，不翻譯）……等等。根據我在柏林皇家圖書館（Royal Library of Berlin）所作的筆記，該館藏有同一著作，附有註解（？），共十三篇（Prapāṭhaka）。……（此處保留梵文原文，不翻譯）……我在別處亦見提及此同一著作。
sakhyas。其真實性受到《遮羅那‧毗喻哈》（Charanavyiiha）的支持，該文獻提及一部《娑摩‧坦特羅》（Sama-tantra），但未提供任何進一步細節。

若問為何所有這些外觀迥異的著作皆歸屬於同一文學時期——即經書時期（Sutra-period），其理由如下：第一，這些著作多數的文體屬於古老的經書文體，例如《泰提利耶‧音律釋》（Taittiriya-pratisakhya）、《迦提亞耶尼耶‧音律釋》（Katyayaniya-pratisakhya）與《四章書》（Chaturadhyayika）¹；第二，手稿稱這些著作為「經」（Sutras）；第三，即便如紹納卡（Saunaka）以混合頌體（Slokas）寫成的作品，亦被引述為「經」²——此稱號絕不會用於《摩奴法論》（Manava-dharmasastra）等著作；第四，這些著作所歸屬的作者，據知亦撰有其他作品，通常以韋伯博士（Dr. Weber）論《娑摩吠陀》（Sama-veda）之有趣文章（《印度研究》Indische Studien，卷一，第48頁）所述文體寫成。值得注意的是，此《娑摩傳承》（Samatantra）被稱為「文法」（Vyakarana）。《頌傳承》（Riktantra）——一部小型音聲學（Siksha）論著——亦獲此稱號（博德利手稿 Bodl. W. 375）。該手稿包含數部與《娑摩吠陀》相關的音聲學小論，但更近似附錄（Parisishtas）形式：其一論「連音分界」（Avagraha），即詞語劃分；其二名為《頌數》（Samasankhya）；其三名為《讚歌收攝》（Stobhanusanhara），開頭語為¹《泰提利耶‧音律釋》各章末尾所附標題為「……如是音律釋經第一問終」（iti pratisakhya-sutre prathamah prasnah samaptah）等語。

2 沙德古魯西希亞（Shadgurusishya）在其《阿努克拉瑪尼註釋》中提及，娑烏那迦（Śaunaka）最初編纂了一部由一千部分組成、類似梵書（Brahmana）的《劫經》（Kalpa-sutra）。^ftcf^ ^^rf ~^k ^V^W^" f%*f。此經後來被他親自銷毀；但他少數流傳下來的作品，以詩體寫成，同樣被稱為經（Sutras）。^pl^3F*l 音韻學（PRATISAKHYAS）。145 經。娑迦羅派（Śākalas）的《音韻學》應以頌體（Ślokas）寫成，卻歸於迦提亞那（Kātyāyana）之師娑烏那迦名下，此點並無矛盾。若此書像《摩奴法典》（Manu）那樣以正規的阿奴濕圖樸頌（Anuṣṭubh-ślokas）寫成，則須將其排除在經書時期之外。然而，頌體與其他古老韻律的混用，比任何其他證據更能顯示從一個時期到另一個時期的過渡，並且完全符合娑烏那迦在印度文學史上所佔據的地位——這一點後文將會說明。

透過比較娑烏那迦（Śaunaka）在其第一部《波羅提娑佉耶》（Pratiśākhya）中關於「式叉」（Śikṣā）的章節，與一般被引為「吠陀支」（Vedāṅga）的簡短頌詩（Śloka）彙編，便能立即察覺新舊頌詩之間的差異。這部已在印度刊行的近代短論，其內容幾乎不超過將濕婆（Śiva）或商羯羅（Śaṃkara）的經句改寫為頌詩的形式。它提及了普拉克里特（Prākṛt）方言，並自稱撰寫於波你尼（Pāṇini）之後，但並非如摩度蘇達那‧娑羅室伐底（Madhusūdana Sarasvatī）所聲稱的那樣，由波你尼本人所作。¹ 然而，令人玩味的是，這部小作品竟能贏得如此盛名——因為熟知諸《波羅提娑佉耶》、並同時引證《娑迦羅》（Śākala）與《泰帝利耶波羅提娑佉耶》（Taittirīya-prātiśākhya）的娑耶那（Sāyaṇa），竟將這部短論視為真正的「吠陀支」。在一部先前提及的《彌曼差》（Mīmāṃsā）著作中，蘇美濕婆羅（Someśvara）的《怛特羅》（Tantra）——又再次：
varttika-tika，似乎這部簡短的《音聲學》（Śikṣā）短論被賦予了比娑烏那迦（Śaunaka）、迦提亞耶那（Kātyāyana）等人更為成熟且顯然更古老的著作還要更高的權威。

除了上述從《泰提利耶森林書》到所謂《吠陀支》所列舉的《式叉》著作外，我們還擁有另一部關於《式叉》的論著，稱為《曼杜基式叉》。¹ 但這部作品很可能也是《經書》時期之後的產物，其重要性僅在於它冠有梨俱吠陀另一學派——曼杜卡亞那²——之名，從而證實了先前的觀點：即每個古老的學派（Śākhā）原本都有其各自的《本集讀本》（Pratiśākhya），儘管其中大多數，連同 ¹ 另一部關於《式叉》的著作，由羅闍‧羅達康塔（Raja Radhakanta）在其《詞典》（Śabda-kalpa-druma）中專論《吠陀支》的文章裡提及，並引用阿摩羅（Amara）與婆羅多（Bharata）為權威來源，梵文引文略。而《沙卡拉本集讀本》（Śākala-prātiśākhya）的註疏似乎也提到了兩種《式叉》。梵文引文略。 ² 曼杜凱亞（Mandukeya）在《沙卡拉本集讀本》中被引用，見 I. 200。
147 他們的咒語文本（Mantra texts），如今或已佚失，或僅以較晚近的形式保存下來，正如在《曼杜基‧式叉》（Manduki-siksha）中所見者。
韻律學或韻律（Chhandas）

第二部吠陀支（Yedanga）學說，即韻律學（Chhandas）或格律，其所處地位與音韻學（Siksha）極為相似。某些後來被採納為格律技術名稱的詞彙，已見於《梨俱吠陀》（Rig-veda）的部分讚歌（Mantras）中，而《梵書》（Brahmanas）裡亦頻繁提及格律。然而，《梵書》中關於格律的論述，通常充滿了教條與神秘的成分，以至於幾乎毫無實用價值。在《森林書》（Aranyakas）與《奧義書》（Upanishads）中，有整章篇幅專門探討此主題。然而，真正有系統地整理這些古老格律的嘗試，仍僅見於《經書》（Sutras）。在《娑迦羅‧波羅提娑佉》（Sakala-pratisakhya）的末尾，有數章論及格律，以壽那迦（Saunaka）慣用的混合頌體（Slokas）風格寫成。此論著早於迦提亞耶那（Katyayana）的作品——後者見於其《薩爾瓦努克拉瑪》（Sarvanukrama）的導論中，因為我們後文將會看到，迦提亞耶那是壽那迦的弟子。關於《娑摩吠陀》（Sama-veda）的格律，我們有《尼達那經》（Nidana-sutra），共分十篇（prapathakas）。該經在解釋所有吠陀格律的性質與不同名稱之後，提供了一種索引（anukramani），用以標示在埃卡哈（Ekaha）、阿希納（Ahina）與薩特拉（Sattra）祭祀中所用讚歌裡出現的格律。至於頻繁被引為吠陀支（Yedanga）的賓伽羅那伽（Pingalanaga）關於韻律學（Chhandas）的著作，它並不自稱年代比 L 2 148 韻律學（CHHANDAS）更為古老。

《大疏》（Mahabhashya）——假設我們承認，這部著名的波你尼（Panini）注釋的作者波顛闍利（Patanjali）與賓伽羅（Pingala）為同一人¹——那麼，賓伽羅既處理俗語（Prakrit）韻律又處理梵語（Sanskrit）韻律一事，便不足為奇。因為我們有迦多衍那‧耶羅盧吉（Katyayana-Yararuchi）為例，他撰寫了波你尼的《釋補》（Yarttikas），年代早於波顛闍利，據說也寫過一部俗語方言語法。然而必須承認，賓伽羅那伽（Pingalanaga）的《韻律學》（Metric）是最可能歸入經書（Sutra）時期的晚期作品之一；不過，並無充分理由將其完全排除於該時期之外，僅因那些涉及吠陀（Veda）中尚未使用之韻律的規則，也被歸於同一位賓伽羅。此外，在《附錄》（Parisishtas）²——一類與經書時期相隔似乎不遠的文獻——中，賓伽羅被引為韻律權威。與賓伽羅論著同屬一類的闡陀（Chhandas）作品——此類作品不限於特定學派（Sakhas），而是針對整個吠陀——另有兩部作品為《娑迦羅‧波羅提娑佉耶》（Sakala-pratisakhya）的注釋者所增補，一部歸於耶斯迦（Yaska），另一部歸於賽陀婆（Saitava）³。然而，這兩部作品目前似乎均已佚失。
1 Colebrooke，《雜論集》，ii. 63。
2 MS. Bodl. W. 466. *T*TTTTT W^l I sn^WTTff^lp* ^NjTTrT^HYw ^T^W I- 參見羅特博士為《尼祿多》所寫之序言，CHEIANDAS. 149 屬於某一學派（Śākhā）的韻律學（Chhandas）著作，與一般專論詩律的論著之間的差異，可由以下實例看出。

根據波顛闍利（Pingala）的經書（Sutras），一首七十六音節的詩律稱為阿提陀哩提（Atidhriti），一首六十八音節的詩律稱為阿提亞濕提（Atyashti）。如今，《梨俱吠陀》（Ṛg-veda）第一卷第一二七首第六頌出現了一首六十八音節的詩句，按理應稱為阿提亞濕提（Atyashti）。然而，根據波顛闍利本人的說法，某些音節可發為兩個音節*，若依循他對此點的規則，同一詩句便由七十六音節而非六十八音節構成。因此，為消除此詩句格律上的不確定性，《娑迦羅‧波羅提娑佉》（Śākala-prātiśākhya）中的〈詩律章〉（Chhandas chapter，位於第十六章（Pāṭala）末尾）宣告：根據娑迦羅（Śākala）或賽屍羅支派（Śaiśira-śākhā）的傳統，此詩句應發音為阿提陀哩提（Atidhriti），即七十六音節。迦多衍那（Kātyāyana）為《娑迦羅本集》（Śākala-saṃhitā）所撰的索引亦給出相同指示。

```markdown **語言的科學**（*Sprachwissenschaft*）是我們這個時代最偉大的學術成就之一。它不僅為我們揭示了語言的本質與發展規律，更為比較神話學（comparative mythology）與宗教學（the science of religion）提供了不可或缺的基礎。正如比較解剖學透過骨骼結構追溯物種的演化，比較語言學也透過詞彙與語法的比較，重建了人類思想與信仰的原始型態。 穆勒指出，神話與宗教的許多核心概念，其實源於語言本身的隱喻與誤解。他稱之為「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）——當原始人類試圖以有限的詞彙表達無限者（the Infinite）時，語言的自然象徵性便逐漸被誤解為字面上的真實存在。例如，希臘神話中的宙斯（Ζεύς）與吠陀（Veda）中的特尤斯（Dyaus）皆源自同一雅利安（Aryan）語根，意為「發光者」或「天空之父」。這種詞源上的對應，證明瞭神話並非純粹的幻想，而是語言演化的必然產物。 在宗教思想的發展階段中，穆勒區分了**單一神教**（henotheism）與**輪換主神教**（kathenotheism）。前者指信徒在特定時刻只崇拜一位至高神，但並不否認其他神祇的存在；後者則是在不同情境下輪流尊崇不同的神祇為最高主宰。這種現象在梨俱吠陀（Rig-Veda）中尤為明顯：當讚歌頌揚阿耆尼（Agni）時，他便被視為眾神之首；但當轉向因陀羅（Indra）或伐樓那（Varuṇa）時，同樣的至高地位又賦予了他們。這種流動性的神觀，既不同於多神教（polytheism）的固定階層，也與一神教（monotheism）的絕對獨一性有別。 穆勒進一步比較了三大語族——雅利安（Aryan）、閃語族（Semitic）與圖蘭語族（Turanian）——的宗教詞彙。他發現，雅利安語族的神名多與自然現象（如天、光、火）相關，而閃語族的神名則更強調權能與契約關係。這種差異反映了不同民族對「無限者」的直觀方式：雅利安人傾向於從自然界的規律性中體悟神聖，閃語族人則從歷史中的神聖幹預來認識上帝。 最後，穆勒強調，宗教學的目標並非評判各宗教的優劣，而是透過比較方法，理解人類如何在不同文化條件下，以語言為媒介，逐步建構對超越者的認知。正如語言科學（the science of language）揭示了語法與詞彙的普遍結構，宗教學也應致力於發現宗教現象中的共通法則——這正是比較神學（comparative theology）的核心任務。 ```
Vyakarana，或曰文法。

第三吠陀支是文法（Vyakarana）或語法。根據印度學者對其文獻的描述，此吠陀學分支應以波你尼（Panini）的語法為代表。然而，此處的矛盾甚至更為明顯。在賓伽羅（Pingala）的《經書》（Sutras）中，吠陀詩律至少是以與非吠陀詩律相同的方式處理。但在波你尼的著作中，特別涉及吠陀語法的規則，僅構成所有其他處理常規或古典語言規則的例外。因此，與其像印度學者那樣，將第三吠陀支學說視為由始於波你尼（Paninyadayah）的語法學家所代表，更準確的說法應是，它是由終於波你尼（Paninyantah）的語法學家所代表。不幸的是，波你尼的著作因其卓越成就而獲得極高聲譽，幾乎取代了他之前所有關於語法的著作；因此，除了早期語法學家的名字和一些特定規則外，此文學分支幾乎沒有留下什麼，除了偶爾出現在《音律釋》（Pratisakhyas）中的內容。波你尼知曉《音律釋》一事，早已由博特林克（Professor Bohtlingk）教授指出；而現在透過比較波你尼的《經書》與《音律釋》的經文，可以證明波你尼大量利用了前人的著作，經常採用他們的確切表述，儘管他僅在這些前人需作為某些規則的權威依據時，才引用他們的名字。

151 個主題，屬於波你尼（Pāṇini）之前的時期：關於 *Unādi* 詞綴的經句（*Sūtras*），以及商塔那恰裡亞（Śāntanācārya）關於重音的經句。*Unādi* 詞綴是指那些由詞根構成名詞的詞綴，這些名詞在慣用意義上使用，而非嚴格遵循其詞根本義。它們之所以被稱為 *Unādi*，是因為在我們現今擁有的經句中，*uñji* 是第一個被提及的詞綴。波你尼熟悉這些構成詞綴的相同編排，這點無庸置疑，因為他對它們使用了相同的技術名稱（*unādi*）。我們不知道這些 *Unādi* 詞綴最初是由誰收集的，也不知道我們現今擁有的《*Unādi* 經句》最初是由誰編纂的。我們只能說，既然波你尼提及它們，並給出了若干關於它們的通則，那麼它們必定存在於他之前。然而，有多少經句存在於波你尼之前，又有多少是在之後添加的，這是一個幾乎無法解決的問題。在它們當前的形式中，這些經句似乎將吠陀詞語視為例外，至少它們時不時會暗示某個派生僅適用於《讚歌》（*Chhandas*）。儘管如此，觀察到一個奇特現象是：由真正的《*Unādi* 經句》所解釋的詞語，絕大多數是吠陀詞語，其中一些甚至是吠陀所獨有的。如果這些經句的作者原本是為《俗語》（*Bhāṣā*）制定規則，那麼他沒有理由對純粹吠陀性質的詞語給予如此顯著的重視。事實上，我相信《*Unādi* 經句》最初僅是為《吠陀》而設，後來才通過添加關於非吠陀詞語構成的規則而得以擴充。最終，非吠陀或世俗（*laukika*）詞語取得了如此主導的地位…… （第 I 卷，第 4 頁，152，《尼祿多》（*Nirukta*））
> 某些僅影響吠陀詞語的規則，實際上不得不作為例外插入。若能在純粹吠陀詞語與從未用於吠陀的詞語之間劃出清晰界線，並將涉及前者的經文與後者分離，我們或許能還原這部有趣著作的原始文本。然而，這項工作所需的印度語言史知識，其廣度與批判性深度，遠非當今任何學者所能掌握。

關於商塔那（Śāntana）的《韻律經》（Phitsūtras），我們對其所屬時代的確定性較低。波你尼（Pāṇini）的著作並未預設對這些經文的認識，且商塔那所使用的語法術語與波你尼所用的不同——根據此一事實，伯林克（Böhtlingk）教授巧妙地推斷，商塔那必定屬於東方的語法學派¹。然而，由於這些經文僅論及重音，而重音僅用於吠陀語言，商塔那著作的主題會引導我們推測他早於波你尼，儘管據此做出進一步推論並不妥當。
尼魯克塔，或《詞源學》。
正如印度學者所言，作為吠陀支的文法（Grammar）以波你尼（Pāṇini）的文法為代表，我們發現尼祿多（Nirukta）同樣僅由一部著作所代表，該著作通常以耶斯迦（Yāska）之名傳世¹。 ¹ 參見 Böhtlingk，《梵語重音初探》（Ein erster Versuch über den Accent im Sanskrit），第 64 頁；以及波你尼（Pāṇini），第 xii 頁。

153 尼祿多（Nirukta）。然而，尼祿多相較於文法學（Vyakarana）有一項優勢：耶斯卡（Yaska）的著作專門處理吠陀（Vedic）語源學。正如我們將波你尼（Panini）的語法視為文法學作為吠陀支（Vedanga）最終定型的著作，耶斯卡似乎也是將吠陀術語的語源詞典學匯集成一部獨立著作的最後幾位作者之一。耶斯卡曾提及尼祿多作者（Niruktakaras），其中有些人必定與耶斯卡本人同樣著名，因為我們發現，即使在《往世書》（Puranas）編纂時期，他們在這方面的功績也未被遺忘。¹ 對於古老吠陀詞語的解釋、語源學及同義詞表達，《梵書》（Brahmanas）提供了非常豐富的材料；除了《儀軌經》（Kalpa）之外，沒有其他吠陀支比尼祿多更有資格被視為奠基於《梵書》之上。整段詩句與讚歌都在其中得到簡短解釋；而《森林書》（Aranyakas）與《奧義書》（Upanishads），若納入考量，將為吠陀語源學提供比尼祿多本身更豐富的來源。《愛多雷耶森林書》（Aitareya-aranyaka）的開頭實際上就是一篇注釋…… ¹ 因此，《毗濕奴往世書》（Vishnupurana, 第277頁，註9）中提到沙卡普尼（Śakapurni）是一位尼祿多作者（Niruktakrit）；但這並非如科爾布魯克（Colebrooke）所推測，認為沙卡普尼與耶斯卡是同一人的理由（Miscell. Essays, i. 15）。事實上，耶斯卡本人曾引用沙卡普尼（Śakapuni），因為沙卡普尼（Śakapurni）與沙卡普尼（Śakapuni）無疑是同一名稱。在後世，耶斯卡與沙卡普尼也被視為不同人物；例如，在一首歸於波羅奢羅（Parāśara）的詩句中（Anukr. Bh.）。
iv. 5. 7.)，見於《大天》（Brihaddevata）。另一部由薩雅納（Sayana）提及的《尼祿多》作者，是斯圖拉什提維（Sthulashthivi）之子，或如亞斯卡（Yaska）所稱，斯圖拉什提維（Sthaulashthivi）。
> 「《尼祿多》是一部作品，其中列舉了許多詞語，而無意將它們連結成句。在那部書中，傳授了傳統數量的詞語，始於 *Gauh*、*gma*，終於 *Vasavah*、*Vajinah*、*Devapatnyah*，其中並無意陳述需被理解的義理；因為書中僅言『如此多為大地之名』、『如此多為黃金之名』，等等。」
此《尼祿多》分為三部分，可由《阿努克拉摩尼迦》的註釋中得見。其中寫道：
